subject: AP World History
theme: Between 1450 and 1750, powerful land-based empires — the Ottoman, Safavid, Mughal, Qing, and Russian — expanded through military conquest, consolidated authority through bureaucratic and religious legitimacy, and managed diverse populations through varying approaches to governance and cultural integration.
core_concepts: The Ottoman Empire (1299–1922) used gunpowder weapons, the devshirme system, and millets to govern a vast, multi-ethnic empire spanning southeastern Europe, the Middle East, and North Africa.
The Safavid Empire (1501–1736) established Twelver Shia Islam as the state religion of Persia, creating a distinct cultural identity that rivaled the Sunni Ottomans.
The Mughal Empire (1526–1857) in South Asia blended Islamic and Hindu traditions, with Akbar's policy of religious toleration contrasting with Aurangzeb's later imposition of orthodox Islamic rule.
The Qing Dynasty (1644–1912) was established by Manchu conquerors who adopted Chinese Confucian governance while maintaining distinct Manchu cultural identity and expanding China's borders.

The Russian Empire expanded eastward across Siberia and Central Asia, using gunpowder technology and Cossack settlers while consolidating power under autocratic tsars like Ivan IV and Peter the Great.
All gunpowder empires used military technology (cannons, muskets) and professional armies to conquer and hold territory, fundamentally changing the relationship between rulers and military power.
Tax farming, tribute systems, and state-controlled economies funded imperial expansion, while elite recruitment systems (devshirme, civil service exams) maintained bureaucratic effectiveness.

key_terms.definition: Turkic Islamic empire (1299–1922) centered in Constantinople that controlled southeastern Europe, the Middle East, and North Africa.
Persian empire (1501–1736) that established Twelver Shia Islam as the state religion, creating a lasting Shia identity in Iran.
Islamic empire in South Asia (1526–1857) founded by Babur, known for cultural synthesis and monumental architecture like the Taj Mahal.
Manchu-ruled dynasty in China (1644–1912) that expanded territory, maintained Confucian governance, and managed ethnic diversity.
Ottoman system of conscripting Christian boys from the Balkans, converting them to Islam, and training them as janissaries (soldiers) or administrators.
Elite Ottoman infantry recruited through the devshirme system, originally enslaved Christians trained as loyal soldiers of the sultan.
Ottoman policy allowing religious minority communities (Christians, Jews) to govern their own internal affairs under their own religious leaders.
Mughal emperor (r. 1556–1605) known for religious toleration, administrative reforms, and his policy of sulh-i-kul (universal peace).
Mughal emperor (r. 1658–1707) who reimposed the jizya tax on non-Muslims and pursued orthodox Islamic policies, contributing to the empire's eventual decline.
Ottoman sultan (r. 1520–1566) who presided over the empire's golden age, expanding territory and patronizing art, architecture, and legal reform.
Russian tsar (r. 1682–1725) who westernized Russia through military, administrative, and cultural reforms, including building St. Petersburg.
Term for the Ottoman, Safavid, and Mughal empires, which relied on gunpowder weapons to build and maintain their power.
System in which the right to collect taxes was sold to private individuals, who paid the state a fixed amount and kept any surplus.
Mughal tax collectors who were granted authority over land revenue collection and often became powerful local elites.

exam_tip: College Board frequently asks you to compare how different empires managed ethnic and religious diversity — be ready to contrast the Ottoman millet system, Mughal religious policies under Akbar vs. Aurangzeb, and Qing approaches to ruling non-Chinese populations.
